speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Dina Brustman. The date is the 26th of November, 1996. I'm interviewing Tola Hauptman in Melbourne, Australia. Would you please tell us your name and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tola Hauptman, T-O-L-A, H-A-U-P-T-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Strosberg, S-T-R-O-S-B-E-R-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you had any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, I-- I [? made ?] [? out ?] one, which sounded Polish, more Polish, like Theofili. But I don't use it very often.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell that for us please?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a bit-- all right. T-H-E-O-F-I-L-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: 23rd of March, 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you?

speaker: interviewer
text: What city were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Warsaw

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you spell that for us please?

speaker: interviewee
text: W-A-R-S-A-V, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the country of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now we're going to ask a few questions about life before the war, for you and your family. Could you describe what your town was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: What mine?

speaker: interviewer
text: What Warsaw was like, the area in Warsaw you lived in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, as a little girl, I-- we used to live about six, seven kilometers from Warsaw. That was called [? Faver. ?] And it was a bit like a country, a little bit. And we had a very nice house, which was divided in three. On the ground floor, we lived. And on the other side, my brother's-- my father's brother, with his family. And all the first floor was occupied by my grandfather, on my father's side. And it was a beautiful garden, with [? all tiles, ?] you know, and beautiful flowers. We had a gardener. We had, also, a area where was-- they were houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because we had-- my father had a business with building material. And he wanted the people who worked for him to have good conditions. He-- so he build them-- it was separate, but sort of one. Like-- how should I call it? Like [POLISH]. That's in Polish. Well, say it in Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a [POLISH]. And there were horses and the drivers, with their families. So I've got a very good recollection of my young years. Because there were kids from one side, and my cousins. And we used to go the forest, and to pick up mushrooms and you know, berries, and all sorts of things. And it was a bit carefree. But also-- I-- I sort of-- I was aware of antisemitists already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even living in that small--

speaker: interviewee
text: Even living in that manner-- [LAUGHS] --or whatever you call it. It was very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very nice. Because next to me, they were some Kordas family. I suppose they were originally from Czechoslovakia. There were two brothers in their family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And of course, they were much poorer than us. But they were great antisemite. And I could feel that they-- not all of them want to play with me. You know? That was first thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Second thing, they rented a-- that was really funny. Because they rented one room to a Jewish family who came from Warsaw. He was a Skoda, or some other firm, engineer. And she was a teacher. But he was dying of TB. And they-- therefore, they decided to live not in Warsaw, but close to. And they had a servant girl who was wonderful with the kids, with their kids. And they-- I mean, they were nice kids. But they looked typical Jewish. And I was their friend. And I didn't especially look Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she used to always laugh from me that I am the one who is Jewish. But her children are all right, only me. You know? So she pinpoint me. And that was already, you know, like a stabbing. You know? I felt it. I was a very sensitive girl. Were there any other incidences that--

speaker: interviewee
text: From before the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: From before the war, while you were still living--

speaker: interviewee
text: In--

speaker: interviewer
text: --in that area, in that manor house on the farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that-- in that area, not-- yes. In-- I went to the state school. Because it was the only one available.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I suppose I was six or seven. And I didn't feel, personally, any antisemitist-- any antisemitism. Because-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --I didn't look different. But they were boys who were from religious homes. And they were beaten.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- did you ever see them beaten?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. And-- and ridicule. They ridiculed them and beat them. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this--

speaker: interviewer
text: --other children at the school? Or who was this, that did that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Other children, certainly. Other children. Yes, and that was really-- it hurt me. Also, I-- I-- I wasn't brought up in a specially-- not a religious home.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would say, a bit assimilated home. But it still hurt me, you know? So that, I can recall from that place.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we moved. Because we had two businesses. My father had two businesses, one in [? Faver ?], and one in Grochow. And we moved to Warsaw. I already-- that was about-- I was 13.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: '37. And I went to the Jewish school. It was the best school in town. And so at that time, I was meeting with my friends from school. Also, I was well aware of what's going on. But nothing drastic, so to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your parents? Did they, at that stage, when you were going to a Jewish school, did they-- were they more involved in the Jewish community in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I know of. My mother had a very enormous family. And she was-- she was only 20 when she married. And when I was born, she was 21.

speaker: interviewer
text: You see? So she was busy visiting the family. And of course, she married well. She, herself, wasn't from a very rich home. And she enjoyed it. So, no. She was carefree, sort of beautiful woman. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --always looked very young, and she enjoyed life. My father, I'm sure he had something. Ah, I can recall also, in [? Faver ?], that was the first time I-- I've seen my father being really courageous. Because we were living on the ground floor. And a thief came through the window. It was during the summer. And my father had, always, a revolver under his pillow. And he was very quick. And he jump over, after him. Not that he stole something. Before he even stole something. And I remember we were trembling. And he-- and the thief lost his things. He left the shoes and the-- the big bag, everything. And-- and everyone congratulated my father. Because my grandfather always knew that oh, he-- he can look after himself and the rest of the family. He was the courageous one. That what I can recall from the earlier years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your living arrangement in-- once you went to Warsaw? Did you live in a flat? Or did you live in a house?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a flat.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a flat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you describe the flat for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a modern flat, nothing very luxurious. But my-- my father never was an ostentatious man. We had two bedrooms, kitchen, and sort of-- from the kitchen, there was a servant's sort of bed, and place, and a place for a table, little table. Big lounge room, and bathroom and a toilet. And a big, very long corridor, I remember. And a balcony.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was this--

speaker: interviewee
text: On the first floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was this seen as a well-to-do sort of flat? Or was it just an ordinary flat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it would be rather well-to-do. Because that was quite a poor area, where I used to live. And that was, yes. It was a better class flat, sort of. And my father, being a builder himself, he knew what is good.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, who lived in the flat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who lived in the flat with you? There was your father, your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my brother, my brother, who was six years younger than me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- what's your brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Henry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Henry. And did you have any other people living in the flat with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The servant girl, yes, to whom I was very attached. And I first learned how to crochet and knitting from her. And I was very interested though, in that sort of thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: I even look how she cooked. You know? I was interested in those things, in manual work.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. I think we might finish this tape now. And we'll start on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're just continuing with this tape. There was a bit of noise from the microphone coming out. So we stopped the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: I see.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were describing your flat, and the people living in the flat. And you were saying how the servant taught you to crochet and work on handicrafts, and that you liked that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe a typical kind of weekend for your family, when you were living in Warsaw? How would you spend the weekend?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was always busy. And if he didn't work, he used to go to his father. And it was the whole gathering, and probably meeting some other businesspeople. My mother was also occupied with her own things. We had our company. And we managed-- we-- I never got bored or something like that. Before you were asking me whether it was a luxurious flat, I suppose so. Because they were all doctors and dentists living there. So I suppose it was a better sort of a flat, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you would say that your family were quite well off?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of that when you were young?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was aware, very much, of that. Because somehow, I used to have poorer friends. And I-- and I never sort of show off. Or I wanted to hide from them that I am richer. It was bothering me. Yes. So we never talk about it. But for instance, I had-- I knew a Jewish girl who was really beautiful, looked like a Pole. But nobody would guess that she was Jewish. And she come from a very poor family. Mother was dead, and there were four girls. And you see, so I always-- I-- I try not to talk about who I am, and how well we are off, or sort of. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would your mother spend her time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her mother was very attached to her family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Especially to her parents. It was, some think, incredible how she was attached to her parents. And all the other children, because they were six daughters and four sons. She come from a orthodox family. She was the only one who was-- my mother wasn't orthodox herself. But she come from orthodox family.

speaker: interviewer
text: In those times, it was very hard for the girl to get married if she didn't have a dowry. Dowry? That it? Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother was so-- such a good daughter. Then she decided to give lessons to some students and make extra money, to help out. Also they-- they weren't sort of-- they had enough to eat. But they weren't very rich. And she-- when she met my father, he was going steady with her older sister. But he met her. And she was really unusually beautiful, with long plaits, and so on. And he fell in love with her. And he decided that she'll be his wife. And it was, you see, in Jewish families, it was unheard of that the younger daughter marries before the older one. So that was a sort of a problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: But my father was so generous that he said, I don't want any dowry. You know? And of course, my grandparents could save some money. So they let her marry. And at the same time, they found for the older-- so-- so [? Cybil-- ?] it was at-- sort of at the same time the wedding of the older sister and my mother. She married very-- a very poor man, who was a electrician but couldn't find a job.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they gave her some money to go to Palestine. And actually, I could have show you the photo of her. They-- they had it hard. They had one son, and they had it hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they went Palestine at that stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: They went to Palestine. And-- because I mean, if someone could have a living in Poland, that was our country. We loved that country.

speaker: interviewer
text: So no one was thinking about emigrating. And she was very unhappy because her whole family was in Poland. And she was the only one who went to Palestine. So that was it. And my mother, she was-- she matriculated. You know? She was intelligent, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she didn't have any special interest in anything. She wanted to have a good time. And that what young girls, or women, mostly wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they have a good time? What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my mother used to-- because my father never had time to go, even for holidays. So she used to go by herself. And she always met people. She was very outgoing and very modest. Because you see, she was from such a orthodox family. For instance, my grandmother was so clever that she sent all the daughters to the high school, Polish sort of-- Polish high school. The intention was that-- that if they go to a Polish school, they won't meet boys. Because they never, never will go with a Polish boy out. And if they would go to a Jewish school, that was when I heard of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I thought, she must have been very clever in her way. You see? Because I don't approve of it. That doesn't matter. But it was really very philosophical. So therefore, they-- they were very modest and very beautiful girls. And they were all married before the war, except one, who was a beauty. You know? She was a brunette, really beautiful.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a pity I didn't prepare-- I've got one photo from before the war. Anyway, so during the liquidation, she went after her parents. She was so attached to her parents. She went to Umschlagplatz after her parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So we'll go back to before the war still. And when did you start to feel things might be changing for you and your family, living in Poland, living in Warsaw? Did you start to feel any changes happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, not me. Because I was already used to sort of antisemitism. I didn't feel it on myself so much. All my friends were-- nearly all my friends were Polish, except friends from the school. And I didn't bother about it. It was my country. I was living there. I was very patriotic. I remember when Pilsudski died. I was crying. You know, I was crying. It was my hero. It was my country, and I loved it. I would never, ever emigrate it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family ever talk about emigrating once things started--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yes, my father--

speaker: interviewer
text: Around what date would that have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am not-- I don't know exactly. Because I heard later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- yeah, it could have been '37, '38. My father predicted, sort of, this thing which came after. He said that there is no place for Jewish people to stay in Poland, because of the growing antisemitism. And also, in connection with Hitler, and what's going on in Germany. So his idea was to emigrate to-- first to Palestine. And if the family doesn't want to, to Australia. Just, I was so surprised. Because he chose Australia, and not America. And he wanted to liquidate. But unfortunately, my grandfather didn't agree to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: His father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he--

speaker: interviewee
text: And he wouldn't go by himself. I mean, with his wife and children.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he had a foreboding about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, when did things actually start to change for your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the war, when the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where you were when the war started for you in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In Grochow, in our flat. I remember the-- I remember the gathering of the intermediate family, like my father's sister and grandfather. And they were talking about it. And-- and my recollection of that time was, because I never really liked school very much, I thought, oh great. I won't go to school anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] So was this-- what date would this have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: That would-- it would have been '39. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there-- did you hear any bombings? Did you see anything in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Before, before I heard any bombings. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- [? sharpen ?] when the war broke out, it was-- '39, in-- in autumn. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, when-- when you-- where were you when you heard the bombs?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in-- in Warsaw, in Grochow. That was our suburb.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in your flat? Were you at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- of course, no, not in school. Because the schools were closed. It was war. War was going on. And we-- we were hiding in cellars.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I-- and then we met all the people who were living-- before the war, we didn't knew-- know any people. And that's how it was in big cities. Nobody knew anybody you know, from the same-- from the-- from the same flats. You know? So you were hiding in cellars underneath the flat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And everybody else from the flats were hiding there too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. They were hiding. And then how we started to talk, and sort of-- but we were full of fear, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- You can remember how you felt?

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt so terrible that I-- I couldn't sort of restrain myself. I started to giggle. I should-- I couldn't stop. That was sort of nervousness. Yes,

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were there for, do you think, in the cellar?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in and out. You know? It was an alarm. And then it was sort of, you know, another alarm said, you can go home. Sort of. And it was a time when my father said, all the men should go--

speaker: interviewer
text: Keep going

speaker: interviewee
text: --to-- --should run away to Russia. Because it's very dangerous.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was after the invasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the invasion, yes. The Germans were in-- in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, maybe we'll wait for that story on-- and put it on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Alright. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll finish the tape here.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, yeah.
